ment and which was satisfactory to
verybody-except Mr.. Ballinger.


The practical question which con-
fronts the settler is suggested by
this extract from Attorney General
Wickersham's opinion:


These certificates having been
issued under an unwarranted con-
tract, cannot be received as credits
for the charges imposed upon the
land under the statutes"


There you are. The poor settler
has gone out with his team in the
broiling sun and has worked day
after day digging and hauling dirt
until he supposes that he has earn
ed hundred dollars. He has plan
ned to pay this money on account
of the next installment due the
government on his farm. He knows
that the money has been earned.
He knows that he has felt the
sweat trickle from his brow and
that his horses have had to be fed
and housed and that he has given
his time and muscle to this labor.


But now comes the benevolent sec


rotary of the interior with a volun-
teer intervention and gratuitous


elgal opinion that the certificate
given by the reclamation service is


no good.


This is what Secretary Ballinger


calls reform He is righting the
wrongs" worked by the Garfield
Newell-Roosevelt administration.
How many settlers are affected
| by this ruling it is impossible to
state Roughly estimated there may
be as many as ten thousand. 1t af-
| fects settlers on all the principal
government reclamation projects


and there are about 30 of these To-


Gated all the way from Washington
state to Arizona and east as far


as Nebraska.


What will the ESTHER do who has


relied on his certificate to pay his


next installment? Already there
are hints in Washington that scalp
ers are on their way to the recla-


nation projects with offers to buy
up these certificates for 80 cents


on the dollar, more or less. The
scalper will tell the farmer that
the certificate is no good and will
show him the opinion of Mr.. Bal
linger and Attorney General Wick
ersham to prove it. Then he will
make the farmer an offer and try
to get the certificate for what he
can. Eventually the certificate will
probably be redeemed at face
value.


The scalper will have the farm
ers money. But the law and Mrs.
Ballinger will be indicated.


Already the water users have
been getting together and passing
resolution, declaring that they re-
gard the cancellation of the coop
erative plan as an unwarranted In
reference and as evidence of his
tility to the general cause of recla
nation


While these resolutions have
been pouring into Washington, Sec
rotary Ballinger has personally au-
thorized the published statement
that it is his intention to remove
from office Frederick n. Newell,
director of the reclamation service.
f. A. Reyes is one of a number of
personal representatives" of the
secretary who are now in the field
